title: I went on 49 first dates 😫 Here's what I learned
text:
I’ve gone on a fair share of dates through 
the years and here are some things - lessons, advice and such, big and small - that I’ve picked 
up along the way. Some things I think are worth looking for, considering and thinking about. 
The good, the bad and the ugly, so… Buckle up. This video relates to the very 
beginning stages of dating, like, we’re talking first dates 
to maybe a couple weeks of dating. Everything mentioned in this video 
is based on my personal experience, conversations I’ve had with friends and some of 
the stuff I’ve read because I’m simply a nerd when it comes to anything related to relationships 
and social psychology and yeah, okay, cool. 1. A connection can’t be forced I don’t think we need to write people off 
immediately, sometimes a connection can develop, but don’t settle for a “maybe” if it’s been 
more than a few dates. Decide after as soon as possible if this is someone you’d actually 
want to pursue. If not, don’t let it linger. Even if you may want it to work, because 
perhaps they seem great in many ways, and on paper, they should be a good match, you 
may think. But you can’t force yourself to feel something for someone, and you shouldn’t 
have to talk yourself into a relationship. The person you’re seeing deserves someone who’s really excited about them, 
and so do you. Leave them be. Love, in my experience, is very specific. 
It’s not exactly something you make happen, you kind of let it happen. It can’t 
be demanded, forced or rushed. What’s that saying? If you have 
to force it, it’s probably sh*t. 2. A first date should be low 
commitment with ample room for escape I think the best first dates are the simple, 
low-commitment ones, especially if you don’t know the person from before. Perhaps you met 
online. Having a whole dinner booked, only to realize five seconds in that this really isn’t 
going to work, is not a fun situation to be in. 3. Physical attraction is important Attraction is one of the foundations 
of a good relationship, and it’s completely valid not to be interested 
in someone simply because you don’t find them to be attractive. It doesn’t 
make you shallow, it makes you human. Now, can attraction grow over time? I’ve 
found some studies

title: I went on 49 first dates 😫 Here's what I learned
text:
ood relationship, and it’s completely valid not to be interested 
in someone simply because you don’t find them to be attractive. It doesn’t 
make you shallow, it makes you human. Now, can attraction grow over time? I’ve 
found some studies supporting that it can, and some that say it can’t. My personal 
experience is that if you find yourself needing to convince yourself that someone is 
attractive in the initial stages of dating, it’s not worth pursuing. They, too, 
deserve someone who finds them desirable. 4. Beware of infatuation Speaking of attraction, beware of infatuation. Some people we meet can be very enchanting. 
They're "everything we've ever wanted". They sweep us off our feet and we drown 
into their pretty eyes. In fact, we drown so deep that our brain gets 
flooded and we can't think clearly. We fool ourselves into believing things are better than they actually are. That they 
are better than they actually are. Now, that’s not to say that feeling very excited 
about someone is necessarily a bad thing, of course we want to feel that. But just be aware 
that your brain can be kind of dumb sometimes. 6. Communicate your intentions I think intentions should be communicated as soon 
as you feel like you know what you want from a person. You don’t need to walk into the date 
having “I want to get married within 6 months” written on your shirt, but I absolutely 
think bringing up the topic is necessary, unless you have a thing for wasting your 
time and being unnecessarily disappointed. 8. Interest is obvious Speaking of fooling yourself, don’t become an 
excuse-finding olympian. His phone didn’t die, he didn’t get attacked by a 
dolphin. He saw your text, he spent plenty of time on his 
phone, and he didn’t call nor reply. Or, perhaps they replied but they were vague. They didn’t include the time and place; 
they got back to you after two days. If it’s really bad, they may 
not reply to your text or call, but they’ll communicate with you by replying to 
your insta story or something like that. That is NOT communication. Leave it be and allow 
space for something better in your life. And remember this: Someone who’s actually into 
you doesn’t need to be reminded of your existence. 15. Take their actions at face value
This one goes hand in hand with my next point which is to take a person's actions at face 
value. It’s one thing to be an understanding, empathetic person, but it’s another to be someone 
who tries to justify

title: I went on 49 first dates 😫 Here's what I learned
text:
ake their actions at face value
This one goes hand in hand with my next point which is to take a person's actions at face 
value. It’s one thing to be an understanding, empathetic person, but it’s another to be someone 
who tries to justify people’s bad behavior. Someone’s childhood trauma is not a ticket for 
them to treat you poorly, and remember that the way someone actually treats you is more important 
than how much you love them or they love you. Also, and you may or may not find this 
to be true, but the red flags you choose to ignore at the beginning will be 
the reason you break up in the end. 11. Don’t be too quick to write people off. This often happens when we go on a date 
with a mental checklist of things we want in a partner. And, yes, knowing what you 
want is crucial for finding what you need, but having a checklist can distract you from being present and from paying attention to 
how things actually feel in the moment. Perhaps they don’t have the exact educational 
background you’d imagined your partner to be, or maybe they’re not much of a cook, but 
how important are these things to you, really? I think it’s worth revisiting 
our mental check-list from time to time. Integration In my experience, the most effective 
way of revisiting it and to process any concerns and emotions we may have 
is by doing it with a therapist, and that’s why BetterHelp is 
sponsoring our video today. BetterHelp offers licensed therapists 
who are trained to listen and help you. Talk to your therapist in a private, 
online environment at your convenience. There is a broad range of expertise in 
BetterHelp’s 20.000+ therapists network that gives you access to help that 
may not be available in your area. You just fill out a questionnaire 
to help assess your specific needs and then you get matched with the 
therapist in as little as 48 hours. Then you schedule secure video and phone sessions, plus you can exchange unlimited messages and 
everything you share is completely confidential. I think getting an outside perspective is 
invaluable; I know some of the things I’m discussing today are things that I’ve come 
to understand with the help of my therapist. With BetterHelp, you can request a new 
therapist at no additional charge, anytime. Join the 3 million plus people who 
have taken charge of their mental health with an experiences BetterHelp therapist. Get 10% off your first month 
at betterhelp.com/lana, that’s better H-E-L-P. Also

text:
apist at no additional charge, anytime. Join the 3 million plus people who 
have taken charge of their mental health with an experiences BetterHelp therapist. Get 10% off your first month 
at betterhelp.com/lana, that’s better H-E-L-P. Also linked 
below in the description box. 12. Be honest with yourself 
about your wants and needs Don’t let anyone else tell you what 
you should like. Do you like it when a man holds the door for you? Or 
pays on the first date? Do you dream of being the breadwinner? 
Don’t let any outside voices interfere with your genuine needs and wants.
 
7. Don’t ignore obvious signs of incompatibility Everyone is different. No one is perfect. 
There will always be disagreements, but you need to know what you’re OK and not OK 
with disagreeing on. What can you live with vs not live with? You need to know your deal 
breakers, and don’t fool yourself thinking you can change someone’s mind on a fundamental 
value or belief they have. Potential is not a person; it’s a figment of your imagination. 
 
5. Don’t judge the new person by the old person It’s not fair to bring emotional baggage 
from a previous date or relationship onto a new one. Sure, we all carry our experiences 
with us, they don’t magically go away because we’re seeing someone new, but itäs not 
fair to let a new person be punished for the last person. Try to go into it 
with a blank slate and don’t generalize. 9. Don’t forget about the friendship part Typically, and I’m aware some people 
are asexual, the difference between romantic and platonic relationships is 
the physical intimacy part. But I think it’s good to ask yourself if you’d be friends 
with this person, romance and hormones aside. Would you want to hang out with them? Do they 
possess qualities you appreciate in a friend? They say love is friendship caught fire. 
Do you feel like it is with this person? 10. Don’t look for someone who 
will create your happiness, look for someone who you’d like 
to share your happiness with. 4. Take precautions 5 things I think are worth mentioning here: - If you met this person online, do 
a video call before meeting them. - Look them up beforehand. I’m 
not encouraging anyone to have a full-blown cyber-stalking session before the 
first date, but look up enough information to make you feel comfortable in meeting 
them and spending time alone with them. - I know distinguishing anxiety from actual danger 
isn’t the easiest thing in the world for everyone. But

title: I went on 49 first dates 😫 Here's what I learned
text:
fore the 
first date, but look up enough information to make you feel comfortable in meeting 
them and spending time alone with them. - I know distinguishing anxiety from actual danger 
isn’t the easiest thing in the world for everyone. But if something feels off with a person, don’t 
write it off as anxiety. It may not be, and it’s not worth finding out in my opinion. A bad 
vibe is a valid reason not to date someone. - Always let someone close to you know who 
you’re seeing and where you’ll be going. - If at any point you feel uncomfortable, don’t be afraid to break the date off 
early. Have a friend call you and fake an emergency or whatever you need to do to get 
out of there. You don’t owe anyone anything. - If you’re someone who is afraid of rejecting 
others, or if you can’t take rejection, please put off dating until you’ve worked on those things. 
You don’t want to be in a situation where you’re too afraid to say no, and you also don’t want to 
be the person who can’t take no for an answer. - First few dates should 
always be in public spaces. If you have anything to add to this 
list, please comment down below. Now I think it’s important to note 
that no one knows what you truly want, need and feel better than you do. So while 
seeking out advice can be immensely valuable, letting some guru or YouTuber on the internet 
dictate your life decisions is quite risky. Because after all, what do they know about 
your life? Do what feels right to you.
